title: Dictionary Methods
beginnerExplanation: Dictionaries have methods for accessing, modifying, and iterating through key-value pairs. Key methods include .get(), .keys(), .values(), .items(), .update(), .pop(), .setdefault(), and .clear(). These methods let you safely access data, merge dictionaries, and iterate without errors.
intermediateExplanation: Accessing dictionary keys directly (dict[key]) crashes if the key is missing. Methods like .get() and .setdefault() provide safe alternatives. .items() lets you loop through both keys and values at once. .update() merges dictionaries. These methods are essential for writing robust code.
advancedExplanation:
my_dict.get(key, default)     # Safe access
my_dict.keys()                # All keys
my_dict.values()              # All values
my_dict.items()               # All (key, value) pairs
my_dict.update(other_dict)    # Merge another dict
my_dict.pop(key)              # Remove & return value
my_dict.setdefault(key, val)  # Get or set default

realWorldAnalogy.title: A Hotel Front Desk
realWorldAnalogy.description: .get() is asking the desk "Is room 404 booked?" and getting a polite "No" instead of an error. .keys() is the room number list. .values() is the guest list. .items() is the full register showing who is in which room. .update() is processing a batch of new reservations.

mentalModel: .get() is asking the desk "Is room 404 booked?" and getting a polite "No" instead of an error. .keys() is the room number list. .values() is the guest list. .items() is the full register showing who is in which room. .update() is processing a batch of new reservations.

executionWalkthrough: 2. Scopes and local namespaces are verified.
3. Target variables are allocated or updated.
commonMistakes: Using dict[key] on a missing key — crashes with KeyError. Use .get() instead.
Modifying a dict while iterating over it — iterate over a copy of .keys() instead.
Forgetting that .pop() removes the key — use .get() if you just want the value.
Assuming dictionaries maintain insertion order in Python < 3.7 (they do in 3.7+).

debuggingMission.scenario: An adventurer is trying to use Dictionary Methods but hit a common pitfall: Using dict[key] on a missing key — crashes with KeyError. Use .get() instead.
debuggingMission.brokenCode:
my_dict.get(key, default)     # Safe access
my_dict.keys()                # All keys
my_dict.values()              # All values
my_dict.items()               # All (key, value) pairs
my_dict.update(other_dict)    # Merge another dict
my_dict.pop(key)              # Remove & return value
my_dict.setdefault(key, val)  # Get or set default
debuggingMission.explanation: Make sure to follow the best practices: Use .get(key, default) instead of dict[key] when the key might not exist.

practiceQuestions.question: What does player.get("mp", 0) return if "mp" is not a key?
practiceQuestions.explanation: .get(key, default) returns the default value if the key does not exist. Here it returns 0 since "mp" is not in the dictionary.

practiceQuestions.question: How do you loop through both keys and values of a dictionary?
practiceQuestions.explanation: .items() returns (key, value) tuples, so you can unpack them directly in the for loop.

challengeQuestions.question: What is the correct keyword or operator most commonly associated with Dictionary Methods?
hints: Look at the syntax summary of the topic.
Check standard keyword usage in Python.

bossBattle.name: The Key Keeper
bossBattle.description: A guardian of a vast vault who only speaks in key-value pairs. You must use the right dictionary method for each request — one wrong access crashes the vault doors.
bossBattle.mechanic: Navigate a maze where each door requires a specific dict method call to open safely.

artifacts.name: The .get() Amulet
artifacts.description: An amulet that always returns a safe default instead of crashing your program.

retentionSystem.revision1: Define the core syntax of Dictionary Methods.
retentionSystem.revision2: Name one common mistake when using Dictionary Methods.

developerMode.industryUsage: Dictionary Methods is widely used in standard library scripts and enterprise Python programs.
developerMode.performanceConsiderations: Optimized by standard compiler rules in modern Python environments.
bestPractices: Use .get(key, default) instead of dict[key] when the key might not exist.
Use .items() for looping: for key, value in my_dict.items().
Use .setdefault() to initialize missing keys with a default value.
Use dict unpacking {**d1, **d2} or .update() to merge dictionaries.
antiPatterns: Using dict[key] on a missing key — crashes with KeyError. Use .get() instead.
Modifying a dict while iterating over it — iterate over a copy of .keys() instead.
Forgetting that .pop() removes the key — use .get() if you just want the value.
Assuming dictionaries maintain insertion order in Python < 3.7 (they do in 3.7+).
developerMode.architectureNotes: Interpreted and executed in the primary application call loop.
